message:
perf(pager): round-robin FastPageArray eviction scan instead of always restarting from index 0

`FastPageArray::remove_any()` evicts an arbitrary page when the
fast cache is full and a new page must take its slot.  The prior
implementation walked `self.pages.iter_mut()` from index 0 on
every call, which under heavy churn meant every eviction tended
to remove the lowest-page-number entry.  That created two
problems:

1. Pathological case under sequential page-number access: each
   eviction targets the page number we just kicked out, so the
   cache thrashes its lowest indices repeatedly while leaving
   the higher indices stable.
2. The scan itself was O(n) from index 0, so under high cache
   pressure the eviction cost grew with the cache size even when
   evictions were happening every other access.

Fix: keep a `next_eviction_scan_start: usize` cursor on the
`FastPageArray` and advance it round-robin.  Each `remove_any()`
call:

  - Starts from `self.next_eviction_scan_start` (clamped to the
    current `pages.len()`).
  - Walks forward to the end, then wraps around to scan the
    front half if no occupied slot was found in the back.
  - Sets the cursor to one past the evicted slot (or 0 when the
    slot was the last entry, to keep the cursor in-range as the
    backing Vec resizes).
  - Resets the cursor to 0 whenever the cache becomes empty
    (count == 0) — across `remove`, `clear`, `cold_reset`, and
    the no-occupied-slot exit path — so we never resume from a
    stale offset after the next admit.

`insert` also seeds `next_eviction_scan_start` to the inserted
index when this is the very first page in a freshly empty cache,
which gives the round-robin a sensible initial start instead of 0
when the first inserted page lives near the end of the backing Vec.

The cursor approach preserves the "arbitrary eviction" contract
of `remove_any()` (callers don't depend on which page is evicted),
while spreading the eviction cost across the cache and breaking
the sequential-access pathology.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
